text_: Picked this up on a whim and finished it in a single sitting, which says both good and bad things. The chemistry between the cynical reporter and the romance author had real charm, and the banter kept me turning pages faster than I meant to. Going in expecting a full novel left me a touch shortchanged, but as a quick, warm escape between heavier reads it absolutely delivers.

text_: Whip Hand brings back Sid Haley, the former steeplechase jockey turned investigator, for a second outing, and it's every bit as gripping as the first. text_: Garmin's BlueCharts are vector-based, meaning they rely on mathematical algorithms to encode all the lines and features that make up a nautical chart, rather than storing a flat picture. In years past I deliberately avoided this type, because vector charts historically sacrificed a lot of fine detail compared to raster charts, which are essentially scanned photographic reproductions of the paper originals. That hesitation no longer applies. With the current BlueCharts, the level of detail has climbed dramatically, now including multiple color schemes, depth shading, and the kind of granular features I used to think only raster could deliver. On the water they've been clear, accurate, and easy to read at a glance. For anyone navigating with a compatible Garmin unit, I recommend these without reservation.

First, the lead actress is undeniably pretty, and the movie leans on that so hard that she and the camera milk it relentlessly, cutting to endless close-ups of her face making charming but utterly pointless expressions for what feels like more than half the runtime. Second, the plot is a shameless retread of better crash-survival movies, complete with the tired twist where you're led to believe the characters survived when they actually didn't. Third, the dialogue is painfully clumsy, stuffed with cliches and delivered like a community-theater rehearsal rather than a professional production. There's almost nothing here that works. Stylish-looking in spots, perhaps, but hollow and derivative to its core. A thorough waste of time.

text_: Inventive, terrific series. Fantasy isn't usually my genre at all, yet this one is so well done that the category stops mattering; you just get pulled right in. The prose is sharper than most, though every now and then a verb tense or participle reads a little oddly, which makes me suspect the author isn't writing in American English. So she may well be perfectly correct grammatically while still sounding slightly unfamiliar to my ear, things like 'swam' rendered differently, or 'leant' instead of 'leaned.' Honestly it adds a bit of charm rather than detracting. None of those tiny quirks dimmed my enjoyment one bit. The world is rich, the characters compelling, and I tore through it.

text_: Above and Beyond plays as a semi-documentary, fact-based retelling of Colonel Paul Tibbets and his role piloting the B-29 that dropped the atomic bomb on Hiroshima in 1945. Throughout, the film keeps its attention fixed on the enormous strain Tibbets, played by Robert Taylor, labored under, and equally on his wife, played by Eleanor Parker, who has to weather long stretches of separation while kept largely in the dark about what her husband is actually doing. As a character study of a man under crushing secrecy and pressure, parts of it are compelling, but the pacing drags and the domestic melodrama wears thin over time. The historical weight is undeniable, yet the execution feels dated and uneven.

text_: Best approach is to set aside the fact that this is War and Peace and simply enjoy it on its own terms as a first-rate piece of Hollywood filmmaking. If what you're after is something that genuinely captures the sprawl and depth of Tolstoy's novel, the 1968 Soviet adaptation is the one to seek out instead. In my view, this 1956 American version is unfairly overlooked, while the 1968 Russian one tends to get more praise than it strictly deserves. It's a real shame nobody managed to fuse the warmth and emotional heart of the 1956 production with the scale and intelligence of the 1968 one; either alone leaves you wishing for the other's strengths. Taken for what it is, though, a handsome, sweeping, thoroughly enjoyable epic.
